g.text: Page Boundary Hazard: Why Unaligned Reads Fault
DANGEROUS: Unaligned Read Crosses Page Boundary
SAFE: Aligned Reads Stay Within Page

div.p: A 16-byte SIMD load starting at 0x4FF0 reads bytes 0x4FF0 through 0x4FFF (in Page 1) AND 0x5000 through 0x500F (in Page 2). If Page 2 is unmapped → SIGSEGV (segmentation fault) The CPU cannot partially complete the load—it's all-or-nothing.

text.tspan: // If Page 2 is unmapped → CPU raises page fault → SIGSEGV
// If Page 2 is unmapped → CPU raises page fault → SIGSEGV

div.p: Key Insight: 4KB pages are multiples of 16 bytes (4096 = 256 × 16). Therefore: a 16-byte aligned read (address % 16 == 0) can never cross a page boundary. The aligned address 0x4FF0 reads 0x4FF0-0x4FFF — entirely within Page 1.

text.tspan: // NEVER touches Page 2 → Safe even if unmapped!
// NEVER touches Page 2 → Safe even if unmapped!

g.text: 0x4FF8 | NOT aligned | DANGER | Crosses to Page 2
0x5000 | 16-byte aligned | SAFE | Fully in Page 2 (if mapped)
0x4F01 | NOT aligned | DANGER | May cross boundary

ul.li: 4096 % 16 = 0 (pages are exact multiples of 16) Therefore: Any 16-byte aligned address A where A mod 4096 ≤ 4080 will have A + 15 in the same page. The aligned read can never span two pages.
